questions.text: What is the primary purpose of CSS3 as discussed in Chapter 4?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To define the structure of the document.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To specify the presentation of elements separately from the structure.

questions.explanation: CSS is used to separate the presentation (style) of a web document from its structural content (HTML).

questions.text: Which attribute is used to declare an **inline style** for an individual element?
questions.explanation: The 'style' attribute is used in HTML5 to apply styles directly to specific elements.

questions.text: When declaring CSS properties, what character follows the property name and what character separates multiple declarations?
questions.explanation: A colon follows the property name (e.g., color: red), and a semicolon separates declarations.

questions.text: Where are **embedded style sheets** typically placed within an HTML5 document?
questions.explanation: Embedded style sheets are placed in the <head> section of an HTML document using the <style> element.

questions.text: What is the correct MIME type for CSS style sheets?
questions.explanation: Style sheets use the 'text/css' MIME type to specify the encoding format.

questions.text: In a CSS rule body, which symbols are used to enclose the declarations?
questions.explanation: The declarations of a CSS rule are enclosed in curly braces { }.

questions.text: In the `font-weight` property, the value **normal** is numerically equivalent to which value?
questions.explanation: Text specified as normal is equivalent to 400, while bold is equivalent to 700.

questions.text: How is a **style class** selector represented in a CSS style sheet?
questions.explanation: Style-class declarations are preceded by a period (e.g., .special).

questions.text: How do you define a selector for an element with a specific **id**?
questions.explanation: ID selectors are defined using the hash (#) symbol followed by the element's ID.

questions.text: Why are relative **font-size** values (like em or %) preferred over absolute values (like points)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Relative values are easier to remember.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Absolute values are not supported by modern browsers.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Relative values provide more flexibility for users to adjust text size for readability.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Relative values ensure the font is always the same exact physical size on all screens.

questions.explanation: Relative values permit flexible viewing and allow users to scale text based on their display and needs.

questions.text: According to the cascading rules, which of the following has the highest precedence?
questions.explanation: Author styles take precedence over user styles, which in turn take precedence over user agent (browser) styles.

questions.text: If a child element and a parent element have conflicting CSS properties, which one generally takes precedence?

choices.label: B
choices.text: The child element's property because it has higher specificity.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The properties are merged and both are applied.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The conflict remains unresolved and the browser chooses randomly.

questions.explanation: Properties defined for child/descendant elements have higher specificity than those for parent/ancestor elements, so the child's styles win.

questions.text: Which `text-decoration` value would you use to strike through text?
questions.explanation: The 'line-through' value applies a strike-through decoration to the text.

questions.text: Which pseudoclass is activated when the user moves the mouse cursor over an element?
questions.explanation: The :hover pseudoclass is used to apply styles when the mouse is positioned over an element.

questions.text: Which of the following is an **absolute-length** measurement?
questions.explanation: Points (pt) are absolute units (1/72 of an inch), whereas px, em, and ex vary based on resolution or font size.

questions.text: In CSS, what is the relative unit **em** based on?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The height of a font's lowercase x.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The height of a font's uppercase M.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The width of the screen in pixels.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The total height of the document.

questions.explanation: The unit 'em' is usually the height of a font's uppercase M.

questions.text: What is the main benefit of using **external style sheets**?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They make the HTML file larger and more complex.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They allow style changes across an entire website by modifying a single file.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are the only way to apply colors to text.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They prevent the browser from caching styles.

questions.explanation: External style sheets facilitate 'skinning' and site-wide consistency because one CSS file can control multiple HTML pages.

questions.text: What are the correct symbols for starting and ending a CSS comment?
questions.explanation: CSS comments use /* to start and */ to end. <!-- --> is used for HTML comments.

questions.text: Which attribute of the `<link>` element specifies the relationship between the HTML document and the external file?
questions.explanation: The 'rel' attribute specifies the relationship (e.g., rel="stylesheet").

questions.text: What does the `z-index` property control?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The horizontal position of an element.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The zoom level of the page.

questions.explanation: Z-index determines the stacking order of elements; higher values appear in front of lower values.

questions.text: When an element is positioned **absolutely**, it is removed from the normal flow and positioned relative to:

choices.label: C
choices.text: The very center of the screen only.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The next element in the document.

questions.explanation: Absolute positioning moves an element relative to the margins of its parent element (specifically the nearest positioned ancestor).

questions.text: Which of the following is an example of an **inline-level element**?
questions.explanation:
, , and are inline-level elements.

,

, and

are block-level elements.

questions.text: What is a characteristic of **block-level elements**?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They do not start on a new line.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are displayed on their own line and have virtual boxes around them.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They are used only for text formatting like bold or italic.

questions.explanation: Block-level elements begin on a new line and occupy the full width available, acting as 'boxes'.

questions.text: How do you specify the location of a background image in CSS?
questions.explanation: The correct syntax is background-image: url(fileLocation).

questions.text: What is the default behavior of the `background-repeat` property?
questions.explanation: The default value for background-repeat is 'repeat', which tiles the image both ways.

questions.text: To keep a background image from moving while the user scrolls the page, which property and value should be used?
questions.explanation: Setting 'background-attachment: fixed' keeps the background image stationary during scrolling.

questions.text: Which property is used to indent the first line of text in an element?
questions.explanation: The 'text-indent' property specifically indents the first line of text.

questions.text: If an element's content is too large for its specified dimensions, which property can add scroll bars?
questions.explanation: The 'overflow' property set to 'scroll' adds scroll bars when content exceeds its container's boundaries.

questions.text: In the CSS Box Model, what is the correct order of layers from the center outwards?
questions.explanation: The box model consists of the Content, then Padding, then the Border, and finally the Margin on the outside.

questions.text: Which property determines the distance between the content inside an element and the edge of the element (the inside of the border)?
questions.explanation: Padding is the space between the content and the border.

questions.text: Which property determines the distance between the element's edge and any outside text or elements?
questions.explanation: Margin is the space outside the element's border.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a valid value for `border-style`?
questions.explanation: Valid values include dotted, dashed, solid, double, groove, ridge, inset, and outset. 'Striped' is not a standard CSS border style.

questions.text: What does the `float` property allow you to do?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Make an element hover in 3D space.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Move an element to one side, allowing other content to flow around it.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increase the z-index of an element automatically.

questions.explanation: Floating moves an element to the left or right, and the surrounding content flows around it.

questions.text: Which media type is used specifically for standard computer screens?
questions.explanation: The 'screen' media type is the most common for web pages viewed on a computer monitor.

questions.text: How do you declare a block of styles that applies only when a document is being printed?
questions.explanation: The @media print rule is used to define styles specifically for printed output.

questions.text: In media queries, which orientation is applied if the screen width is greater than the height?
questions.explanation: If width > height, the orientation is 'landscape'. If height > width, it is 'portrait'.

questions.text: Which property can be used to hide an element completely so it is not rendered at all?
questions.explanation: Setting 'display: none' prevents the element from being rendered or taking up space.

questions.text: Where are **User Style Sheets** defined?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Linked in the <head> of the HTML document.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Defined within the browser's options or settings.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hard-coded into the operating system kernel.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Provided by the web server via a database.

questions.explanation: User style sheets are external files set in the user's browser options to reflect their personal viewing preferences.

questions.text: Which type of font size measurement will yield to a user-defined style instead of overriding it?
questions.explanation: Relative font sizes allow user style sheets to scale the text, whereas absolute sizes override them.

questions.text: What is the purpose of a **CSS validator**?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To check if the website is hosted on a fast server.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To ensure that CSS code is correct and compliant with browser standards.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To convert HTML into CSS automatically.

choices.label: D
choices.text: To detect viruses in the style sheet.

questions.explanation: Validators (like W3C's) help ensure your code follows CSS rules and works correctly on compliant browsers.

questions.text: Which generic font family would be a safe fallback if a specific font like 'Times New Roman' is not available?
questions.explanation: Serif is the generic font family for fonts with small lines (serifs) at the ends of characters, like Times New Roman.

questions.text: The CSS property `font-style` is primarily used to set text to which of the following?
questions.explanation: Font-style allows settings like 'none', 'italic', or 'oblique'.

questions.text: Which property allows you to center text within a block-level element?
questions.explanation: Text-align property with the value 'center' is used for alignment.

questions.text: If you want to apply a purple color to multiple unrelated elements across your page, which selector type is most appropriate?
questions.explanation: Class selectors can be applied to any number of elements, whereas IDs should be unique to one element.

questions.text: What is the result of applying `position: relative` to an element?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The element is positioned relative to its normal position in the document flow.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The element is fixed to the top-left of the screen.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The element behaves exactly like a block-level element with no changes.

questions.explanation: Relative positioning moves an element relative to where it would normally be in the document flow.

questions.text: In the context of the box model, which property can take values like 'thin', 'medium', or 'thick'?
questions.explanation: Border-width can be set using lengths (px, pt) or predefined keywords like thin, medium, or thick.

questions.text: Which media feature query would you use to check the aspect ratio of the output device's screen?
questions.explanation: The 'device-aspect-ratio' feature checks the ratio of device-width to device-height.

questions.text: What is the relationship between `bold` and the numerical `font-weight` scale?
questions.explanation: Numerically, 400 is normal and 700 is bold.

questions.text: Which element is used as a generic **block-level** grouping element?
questions.explanation: <div> is a block-level grouping element; <span> is its inline-level counterpart.

questions.text: Which measurement unit is based on the height of a font's lowercase 'x'?
questions.explanation: The 'ex' unit is defined as the height of the lowercase 'x' for the current font.
